chapter_summary: The American common school was founded on the belief that a shared curriculum of facts, myths, and values was essential for creating a unified citizenry capable of sustaining a republic. While modern reforms focus on technical competence and global economic competition, the nation's founders viewed public education primarily as a tool to prevent the internal factionalism that historically destroyed republics like ancient Rome.

claims.text: In the late 19th century, American children across the nation were learning many of the same things despite the absence of an official national curriculum.
claims.source_passage: All across the nation in 1893, whether in a quick-built gold-rush town like Hecla (now a ghost town) or in the multi-classroom schools of larger towns, American children were learning many of the same things.

claims.text: The 19th-century American education system achieved curriculum standardization through a 'benign conspiracy' among schoolbook writers.
claims.source_passage: America had no official national curriculum, but it had the equivalent: a benign conspiracy among the writers of schoolbooks to ensure that all students would learn many of the same facts, myths, and values and so grow to be competent, loyal Americans.

claims.text: Early American history lessons were designed to highlight principles of freedom and provide illustrious examples of patriotism.
claims.source_passage: History lessons might have been based on Salma Hale’s History of the United States, which, like other history books of the time, aimed “to exhibit in a strong light the principles of religious and political freedom which our forefathers professed ...and to record the numerous examples of fortitude, courage, and patriotism which have rendered them illustrious.”

claims.text: Modern educational policy is primarily driven by anxiety regarding students' technical competence in the global economy.
claims.source_passage: In our own era, worried policy makers have fixed their eyes on our underperforming schools and devised new laws and free-market schemes to make our students more competent participants in the global economy.

claims.text: Modern educational reformers believe technical problems in education can be solved by identifying 'islands of excellence' or implementing free-market schemes.
claims.source_passage: Reformers argue that these technical problems can be solved, and they point to exceptional islands of excellence among our public schools even in the midst of urban poverty.

claims.text: The American founders' primary motivation for supporting public schools was the survival of the Republic, rather than just technical competence.
claims.source_passage: The reason that our eighteenth-century founders and their nineteenth-century successors believed schools were crucial to the American future was not only that the schools would make students technically competent. That aim was important, but their main worry was whether the Republic would survive at all.

claims.text: Republics are historically among the least stable forms of government and are prone to collapse from internal antagonisms.
claims.source_passage: Thomas Jefferson, John Adams, James Madison, and Franklin and their colleagues consistently alluded to the fact that republics have been among the least stable forms of government and were always collapsing from their internal antagonisms and self-seeking citizens.

claims.text: Internal factions represent the most significant 'mortal danger' to the American Republic.
claims.source_passage: Franklin and Benjamin Rush from Pennsylvania, and Madison, Jefferson, and George Washington from Virginia and their colleagues thought that a mortal danger lay in our internal conflicts—Germans against English, state against state, region against region, local interests against national interests, party against party, personal ambition against personal ambition, religion against religion, poor against rich, uneducated against educated.

claims.text: The structural checks and balances of the national government are insufficient on their own to preserve the Republic.
claims.source_passage: To keep that from happening, we would need far more than checks and balances in the structure of the national government.

claims.text: Preserving the Republic requires a 'special new brand of citizens' who prioritize the common good over local interests.
claims.source_passage: We would also need a special new brand of citizens who, unlike the citizens of Rome and other failed republics, would subordinate their local interests to the common good.

claims.text: Republican government requires a higher degree of human virtues, such as esteem and confidence, than any other form of government.
claims.source_passage: Republican government presupposes the existence of these qualities in a higher degree than any other form.

claims.text: Uncontrolled hostile factions between the rich and poor or the educated and uneducated would subvert the common good and lead to military dictatorship.
claims.source_passage: If uncontrolled, these hostile factions would subvert the common good, breed demagogues, and finally turn the Republic into a military dictatorship, just as in ancient Rome.

claims.text: Common schooling is the only method to create the virtuous, civic-minded citizens necessary for the American experiment.
claims.source_passage: Our early thinkers about education thought the only way we could create such virtuous, civic-minded citizens was through common schooling.

claims.text: The school serves as the primary institution for transforming future citizens into loyal Americans by teaching common knowledge, virtues, and language.
claims.source_passage: The school would be the institution that would transform future citizens into loyal Americans. It would teach common knowledge, virtues, ideals, language, and commitments.

claims.text: To ensure social fairness and equality of opportunity, children of all economic classes must attend the same school.
claims.source_passage: He outlined a system of elementary schooling that required all children, rich and poor, to go to the same school so that they would get an equal chance regardless of who their parents happened to be.

claims.text: A common core curriculum in early grades is essential for fostering patriotism and enabling effective participation in the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: And the early grades were to have a common core curriculum that would foster patriotism, solidarity, and civic peace as well as enable effective commerce, law, and politics in the public sphere.

claims.text: The American political experiment depends on a common public sphere that can only be created through public schools.
claims.source_passage: Like Madison and Lincoln, these New Yorkers understood that the American political experiment, which left everyone undisturbed in their private sphere, depended on a common public sphere that only the schools could create.

claims.text: Physical force is incapable of producing national union; only moral and intellectual cultivation can sustain it.
claims.source_passage: Physical force may generate hatred, fear and repulsion; but can never produce Union. The only salvation for the republic is to be sought for in our schools.

claims.text: The success or failure of the American experiment in self-government is wholly dependent on the cultivation of the minds of the young in schools.
claims.source_passage: On the careful cultivation in our schools, of the minds of the young, the entire success or the absolute failure of the great experiment of self government is wholly dependent

claims.text: The American Union must be sustained by the moral and intellectual powers of the community because physical force cannot produce lasting union.
claims.source_passage: That Union is and must be sustained by the moral and intellectual powers of the community, and every other power is wholly ineffectual. Physical force may generate hatred, fear and repulsion; but can never produce Union.

claims.text: The primary salvation for the American republic is to be found in its schools.
claims.source_passage: The only salvation for the republic is to be sought for in our schools.

claims.text: Early American educational policy aimed to provide common textbooks to ensure students became independent citizens rather than religious or political partisans.
claims.source_passage: The aim, they said, was not to 'make our children and youth either partisans in politics, or sectarians in religion; but to give them education, intelligence, sound principles, good moral habits, and a free and independent spirit; in short, to make them American free men [and women] and American citizens'

claims.text: Influential early textbook writers like Noah Webster and William McGuffey intended to create a common language and shared loyalty to the public good.
claims.source_passage: Exactly the same sentiments animated the great writers of our earliest textbooks, including Noah Webster and William McGuffey. They aimed to achieve commonality of language and knowledge and a shared loyalty to the public good.

claims.text: The American common school prioritized common knowledge, virtue, and allegiance to the community over individual or local origins.
claims.source_passage: The center of its emphasis was to be common knowledge, virtue, skill, and an allegiance to the larger community shared by all children no matter what their origin.

claims.text: The common school model allowed for local curricular diversity while maintaining a primary devotion to national identity.
claims.source_passage: Diverse localities could teach whatever local knowledge they deemed important, and accommodate themselves to the talents and interests of individual children; but every school was to be devoted to the larger community and the making of Americans.

claims.text: By the 1830s, American education had successfully made Americans more loyal to the common good than their European counterparts.
claims.source_passage: Tocqueville took special note of how much more loyal to the common good Americans were than his factious fellow Europeans.

claims.text: Education in the United States is historically directed toward political life, whereas European education was historically directed toward private life.
claims.source_passage: In the United States the general thrust of education is directed toward political life; in Europe its main aim is to fit men for private life

claims.text: American education historically served to counteract the natural defects and imperfections of democracy.
claims.source_passage: the Americans had made great and successful efforts to counteract these imperfections of human nature and to correct the natural defects of democracy.

claims.text: Educational reformers who believe they are independent of theory are often unknowingly controlled by the ideas of past theorists.
claims.source_passage: Practical men who believe themselves to be quite exempt from any intellectual influence are usually the slaves of some defunct [theorist].

claims.text: Cultural assimilation in America is achieved through shared language, manners, and 'argot' rather than the suppression of religious symbols.
claims.source_passage: This girl sounded just like my sixteen-year-old granddaughter. Her intonations were the same... her vocabulary larded with 'awesome' and 'fantastic'... this girl is wearing a headscarf, but she is American to the core.

claims.text: France's policy of barring religious headscarves in schools indicates a failure to understand key democratic principles shared by French philosophers and American Founders.
claims.source_passage: I think that France has failed to learn important principles from its own philosophers and our brilliant American Founders.

claims.text: France's decision to bar Muslim girls from wearing headscarves to school represents a failure to apply the principles of its own philosophers and the American Founders.
claims.source_passage: I think that France has failed to learn important principles from its own philosophers and our brilliant American Founders.

claims.text: The American 'civic theory of live and let live' is the most successful political idea in history for enabling diverse groups to live together in harmony.
claims.source_passage: the underlying civic theory of live and let live that, in human history, has proved to be the most successful political idea yet devised to enable people of different tribes to live together in safety and harmony

claims.text: American students are not being taught the underlying civic theory of the Founders effectively.
claims.source_passage: My guess, based on consistent reports of our students’ lack of civic knowledge, is that she had not been taught very well.

claims.text: A lack of civic and general knowledge is the most significant deficit in the education of American students.
claims.source_passage: A lack of knowledge, both civic and general, is the most significant deficit in most American students’ education.

claims.text: The reading ability of American twelfth graders has been on a long-term downward trend.
claims.source_passage: The trend line is a gentle downward slope.

claims.text: A decline in reading ability necessarily entails a decline in general knowledge.
claims.source_passage: This decline in reading ability necessarily entails a decline in general knowledge

claims.text: By the twelfth grade, general knowledge is the primary factor determining a student’s level of reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: by the twelfth grade, general knowledge is the main factor determining a student’s level of reading comprehension.

claims.text: To understand any piece of writing, a reader must already possess knowledge about its subject matter.
claims.source_passage: To understand a piece of writing (including that on the Internet and in job-retraining manuals), you already have to know something about its subject matter.

claims.text: Children need to be explicitly taught the knowledge silently assumed in reading and writing.
claims.source_passage: Fortunately, even in the late 1980s and early 1990s many educators accepted my argument that children need to be explicitly taught the knowledge silently assumed in reading and writing.

claims.text: Schools cannot take a laissez-faire attitude toward the content of early schooling if they want students to read and write well.
claims.source_passage: I realized that if we want students to read and write well, we cannot take a laissez-faire attitude to the content of early schooling.

claims.text: The content of early schooling must be specifically defined to produce competent readers and writers.
claims.source_passage: In order to make competent readers and writers who possess the knowledge needed for communication, we would have to specify much of that content.

claims.text: The knowledge required for reading and writing is largely traditional because it is long-lasting and intergenerational.
claims.source_passage: because the assumed knowledge required for reading and writing tends to be long lasting and intergenerational, much of that content would have to be traditional.

claims.text: Anti-traditional school reforms labeled 'left-wing' have the unintended consequence of depressing equality of educational opportunity.
claims.source_passage: anti-traditional school reforms that were labeled left-wing had the unintended consequence of depressing equality of educational opportunity

claims.text: Educational policy should be based on nitty-gritty pragmatism rooted in the science of reading rather than ideological labeling.
claims.source_passage: Left and right both need to adopt a nitty-gritty educational pragmatism that is based on science and the nature of reading and writing

claims.text: The success of Core Knowledge schools has occurred despite intense opposition from educational administrators and experts.
claims.source_passage: The news about their practical success has spread by word of mouth despite intense opposition from educators and administrators.

claims.text: The first implementation of Core Knowledge schools in Florida and the South Bronx was enormously successful but largely ignored by educational experts.
claims.source_passage: Both schools were enormously successful but—for reasons that I will explore later—largely ignored by educational experts (though not by the popular press).

claims.text: The chief factor in the comprehension of language is relevant knowledge about the topic at hand.
claims.source_passage: Back in the 1970s, when I was doing research on reading and writing, the field of psycholinguistics was just beginning to emphasize that the chief factor in the comprehension of language is relevant knowledge about the topic at hand.

claims.text: Comprehension is not just a matter of knowing individual words.
claims.source_passage: Yet the words are familiar enough. Comprehension is not just a matter of knowing words.

claims.text: Comprehension depends on constructing a 'situation model' that enables the reader to supply essential information not explicitly stated.
claims.source_passage: Comprehension depends on constructing a mental model that makes the elements fall into place and, equally important, enables the listener or reader to supply essential information that is not explicitly stated.

claims.text: Communication depends on both writer and reader sharing a basis of unspoken (tacit) knowledge.
claims.source_passage: This means that communication depends on both sides, writer and reader, sharing a basis of unspoken knowledge.

claims.text: The major reason for low reading comprehension scores among American students is a 'knowledge deficit.'
claims.source_passage: Their knowledge deficit is the major reason their reading comprehension scores remain low.

claims.text: Specific subject-matter knowledge across broad domains is the key to language comprehension and the ability to learn new things.
claims.source_passage: Specific subject-matter knowledge over a broad range of domains is the key to language comprehension and to a broad ability to learn new things.

claims.text: A shortfall in conveying enabling knowledge is a chief cause of the failure to offer equal educational opportunity to all children.
claims.source_passage: A shortfall in conveying this enabling knowledge is a chief cause of our educational shortcomings—including our glaring failure to offer equal educational opportunity to all children.

claims.text: Initiation into the public sphere does not require students to reject their private sphere.
claims.source_passage: An initiation into this public sphere does not require rejecting the private sphere that nurtured them.

claims.text: The declining performance on reading tests in the eighth and twelfth grades is evidence that students do not gain shared knowledge naturally through their environment.
claims.source_passage: The disastrously low and still declining performance on reading tests in the eighth and twelfth grades is decisive evidence that they do not gain this enabling knowledge, despite our schools’ strong emphasis on reading under the No Child Left Behind act.

claims.text: The failure of the No Child Left Behind act is primarily due to the scientifically inadequate 'how-to' theory of reading comprehension used in schools.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, the disappointing results of that law owe less to its defects than to flaws in the scientifically inadequate how-to theory of reading comprehension, on which so much time is being wasted in the schools to fulfill the law’s provisions.

claims.text: Membership in the public sphere requires mastery of formal codes of speech and the tacit knowledge that makes that speech intelligible.
claims.source_passage: Membership in this public sphere means mastery of the formal codes of speech and of the tacit knowledge that makes formal speech intelligible—shared information about football, civics, and so on.

claims.text: Students do not gain essential shared knowledge simply through peer interaction or popular culture.
claims.source_passage: Some educators have wishfully asserted that our students will gain this shared knowledge through their pores by interacting with their peers and listening to tunes on iPods. The disastrously low and still declining performance on reading tests in the eighth and twelfth grades is decisive evidence that they do not gain this enabling knowledge.

claims.text: The shared background knowledge required for literacy is relatively stable and changes slowly across generations.
claims.source_passage: The tacit, intergenerational knowledge required to understand the language of newspapers, lectures, the Internet, and books in the library is inherently traditional and slow to change.

claims.text: Achieving democratic goals like high universal literacy necessitates educational traditionalism in schools.
claims.source_passage: Logic compelled the conclusion that achieving the democratic goal of high universal literacy would require schools to practice a large measure of educational traditionalism.

claims.text: Shared language and knowledge are the primary agents for creating a sense of belonging in large societies.
claims.source_passage: In large human collectivities, shared language and the accompanying shared knowledge are the chief agents of the sense of belonging.

claims.text: Ethnicity is an artificially constructed identity based on language and school-promulgated knowledge rather than something innate or racial.
claims.source_passage: Too often, ethnicity is thought of as something deeper than publicly shared language and knowledge—something quasi-racial. But school-based Hispanic ethnicity, say, is no more natural or innate than school-based American ethnicity.

claims.text: Assimilation into the public sphere is a technical necessity for effective education, not just an ideological goal.
claims.source_passage: “Assimilation” sounds like a political or ideological goal, but it is also very much a technical necessity for effective education, because reading, writing, speaking, listening, teaching, and learning require the possession of the shared tacit knowledge of the language community.

claims.text: Universal and complete education can eliminate artificial class distinctions by expanding the cultivated class.
claims.source_passage: The spread of education, by enlarging the cultivated class or caste, will open a wider area over which the social feelings will expand; and, if this education should be universal and complete, it would do more than all things else to obliterate factitious distinctions in society.

claims.text: Standard American English was an artificial construction formed by dictionaries and early schoolmasters.
claims.source_passage: Standard American English... was formed with the help of Dr. Johnson’s dictionary and schoolmasters like Blair, Webster, and McGuffey.

claims.text: Education has the power to enlarge the cultivated class and obliterate factitious social distinctions between property and labor.
claims.source_passage: The spread of education, by enlarging the cultivated class or caste, will open a wider area over which the social feelings will expand; and, if this education should be universal and complete, it would do more than all things else to obliterate factitious distinctions in society.

claims.text: Twentieth-century educators disastrously overturned the principles of the American founding educational thinkers.
claims.source_passage: My purpose is not to assert simply that the good done by Webster, McGuffey, and Mann was disastrously undone by the twentieth-century educators who overturned their principles. Though that is in fact the case, I will not hark back to a golden age that never existed.

claims.text: Modern knowledge of language, learning, and successful international school systems surpasses that of previous generations.
claims.source_passage: We know more than any of our predecessors did about language and learning. We know more about the policies fostering high competence in the most successful national school systems elsewhere in the world.

claims.text: The failure of inner-city schools is caused by content poverty and curricular incoherence rather than solely poverty and family neglect.
claims.source_passage: We know more about why our inner-city schools have been failing: not simply because of high poverty and family neglect but also because of content poverty and curricular incoherence.

claims.text: The schoolbooks of Noah Webster and William McGuffey contained significant doses of bigotry, nationalism, and sectarian religiosity.
claims.source_passage: Such an aim would merely open an endless debate about the unfortunate way things really were during the WASPish days of Noah Webster and William McGuffey, whose schoolbooks served up significant doses of bigotry, pugnacious nationalism, and sectarian religiosity.

claims.text: The fundamental aim of American schooling must be to develop the common public sphere to safeguard the heterogeneous private sphere.
claims.source_passage: the larger aim upon which American schooling needs to be founded is the same as in Webster’s day—to develop the common public sphere in order to liberate and safeguard the heterogeneous private sphere.

claims.text: Offering common curricular topics enables a diverse immigrant population to communicate and act as a unified republic.
claims.source_passage: if schools did so, they felt, we would be able to communicate with one another, act as a unified republic, and form bonds of loyalty and patriotism among our citizens.

claims.text: In the late 18th century, a nationwide core of commonality in education was a consensus view among leading American thinkers.
claims.source_passage: This idea of commonality in the early curriculum was far from a radical idea in 1797, when the American Philosophical Society published the prize essays. It was already the consensus view of such earlier writers as Jefferson and Rush.

claims.text: The extraordinary diversity of American national origins makes a core of uniform schooling peculiarly necessary.
claims.source_passage: He reasoned that the extraordinary diversity of our national origins argued powerfully for a core of uniformity in our schooling: 'I conceive the education of our youth in this country to be peculiarly necessary in Pennsylvania while our citizens are composed of the natives of so many different kingdoms in Europe.'

claims.text: Creating a homogeneous mass of people through education facilitates uniform and peaceable government.
claims.source_passage: Our schools of learning, by producing one general and uniform system of education, will render the mass of the people more homogeneous and thereby fit them more easily for uniform and peaceable government.

claims.text: Early American educational thinkers supported the private development of individual talent and happiness alongside public civic goals.
claims.source_passage: They were simply not focusing on the private dimension of schooling— the development of personal talent and individuality—an aim of private cultivation that they fully supported.

claims.text: The primary emphasis of early American educational thought was on a child's future public responsibilities as a citizen.
claims.source_passage: But their main emphasis was on the child’s future public responsibilities as a citizen of the Republic.

claims.text: The Founders' use of the term 'homogeneity' was not an advocacy for 'lock-step education' but a focus on preparing students for the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: The word “homogeneous” might horrify many who have been taught to honor individual differences. But these inaugural thinkers were not advocating what some might reflexively (and mistakenly) label “lock-step education.” They were simply not focusing on the private dimension of schooling— the development of personal talent and individuality—an aim of private cultivation that they fully supported.

claims.text: The founding educational thinkers fully supported the private dimension of schooling, including the development of personal talent and individuality.
claims.source_passage: Many of their statements show that they were concerned with children’s individual talents, and even with their private happiness, as well as their virtues.

claims.text: Labeling commonality as 'un-American' because it conflicts with multiculturalism is an oversimplification.
claims.source_passage: Today, the idea of commonality is labeled un-American because it conflicts with our more capacious idea of American identity... This is an oversimplification.

claims.text: The 'salad bowl' and 'melting pot' metaphors for American identity are not mutually exclusive.
claims.source_passage: Salad bowl and melting pot are not mutually exclusive concepts—and they cannot be if we are to have a bowl to hold the salad.

claims.text: The distinction between public and private spheres is fundamental to the American founding concept.
claims.source_passage: Fundamental to this founding concept is the distinction between the public and private spheres of life.

claims.text: The public sphere functions as a 'melting pot' where common laws, language, and shared unspoken knowledge are required for mutual understanding.
claims.source_passage: This public sphere is the melting pot, where common laws, a common language, and shared unspoken knowledge and values are needed to make the uttered language comprehensible.

claims.text: The nineteenth-century common-school movement was an authentic extension of the founding ideas of Jefferson, Rush, and Webster.
claims.source_passage: The common-school movement succeeded in establishing the conscious goal of preparing Americans for the public sphere and was thus an authentic extension of the founding ideas of Jefferson, Rush, and Webster.

claims.text: In the early grades of schooling in a democracy, the public sphere should take priority over individual interests.
claims.source_passage: In the early grades of schooling in a democracy, the public sphere should take priority.

claims.text: Democracies and modern economies cannot function properly without citizens who share a common foundation of arithmetic, alphabet, grammar, and facts.
claims.source_passage: Most modern nations impose that kind of compulsory early education because neither a democracy nor a modern economy can function properly without loyal and competent citizens able to communicate with one another.

claims.text: The early curriculum should be viewed as concentric circles, with common core knowledge at the center.
claims.source_passage: Under this founding conception, the early curriculum can be viewed as a set of concentric circles. At the core are the knowledge and skills all citizens should have.

claims.text: Core educational elements must be set forth specifically grade-by-grade to ensure school time is effective and all students are on an equal footing.
claims.source_passage: These need to be set forth specifically, grade by grade, so that one grade can build cumulatively on the prior one, allowing school time to be used effectively and putting all students in a given grade-level classroom on an equal footing.

claims.text: The community-centered common school is a conception that needs to be updated and reintroduced.
claims.source_passage: The community-centered common school is a conception that needs to be updated and reintroduced.

claims.text: The common school movement declined in the early twentieth century.
claims.source_passage: In it the authors trace the decline of “the common school” and the common-school movement, now relegated in our educational histories to the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries.

claims.text: The central core elements common to all citizens of the Republic must be imparted most clearly and explicitly.
claims.source_passage: at must be imparted most clearly and explicitly are the central core elements common to all citizens of the Republic.

claims.text: Core curriculum elements need to be set forth specifically, grade by grade, so that learning can build cumulatively.
claims.source_passage: These need to be set forth specifically, grade by grade, so that one grade can build cumulatively on the prior one

claims.text: The idea of commonality in early schooling is fundamental to the educational ideas of Jefferson, Washington, Rush, Knox, Smith, and Mann.
claims.source_passage: This idea of commonality in early schooling is fundamental to the educational ideas of Jefferson, Washington, Rush, Knox, Smith, and Mann.

claims.text: There was nothing inevitable in the retreat from commonality in American education; it was contingent.
claims.source_passage: There was nothing inevitable in the retreat from commonality. What happened was quite contingent.

claims.text: Advocates of new educational theories attacked the common-school tradition shortly before the beginning of the twentieth century with nationalistic self-righteousness.
claims.source_passage: Shortly before the beginning of the twentieth century, with a ferocity fueled by nationalistic self-righteousness, advocates of a rival set of ideas attacked the common-school tradition—and even the idea of tradition itself.

claims.text: The child-centered school model inverts the educational focus from shared knowledge to the individual child's interests, talents, and needs.
claims.source_passage: Instead of placing at the center of emphasis and concern the knowledge and skill that all citizens should possess, the new center was to be the individual child and his or her interests, talents, and needs.

claims.text: The indispensable principle of the child-centered approach was that specific subject matter was no longer to be determined in advance.
claims.source_passage: the indispensable principle of the new approach was that specific subject matter was no longer to be determined in advance.

claims.text: The child-centered idea became dominant in American teacher-training institutions by the 1920s and 1930s.
claims.source_passage: The new conception quickly became dominant: it was promulgated with vigor in our teacher-training institutions by the 1920s and 1930s.

claims.text: The child-centered movement has maintained a partisan refusal to acknowledge that a subject-centered common school could be child-centered and loving.
claims.source_passage: It has held to a partisan refusal to acknowledge that a subject-centered common school might possibly be child-centered and loving in the manner of its teaching.

claims.text: The child-centered idea made controversy over specific curriculum substance unnecessary by treating content as secondary or trivial.
claims.source_passage: One consequence of the child-centered idea was to make controversy over the specific substance of the curriculum unnecessary. The choice of content would be secondary, even trivial

claims.text: No knowledgeable cognitive scientist agrees with the theory that schools should focus on critical thinking skills rather than facts.
claims.source_passage: This succinctly summarizes the theory now taught in our schools of education—although no knowledgeable cognitive scientist agrees with it.

claims.text: The child-centered idea and the how-to idea together constitute 'the anti-curriculum movement.'
claims.source_passage: Taken together, the child-centered idea and the how-to idea constitute the modern tradition of American education. ... it could more accurately be called “the anti-curriculum movement.”

claims.text: Current language-arts textbooks lack any guiding principles of shared knowledge compared to historical texts like Webster and McGuffey.
claims.source_passage: A look at current language-arts textbooks reveals a stark contrast to Webster, McGuffey, and their descendants, not just in bulk and glitziness but in their lack of any guiding principles of shared knowledge.

claims.text: Commonality in the curriculum for early grades collapsed in the United States by the 1950s.
claims.source_passage: As late as 1940, some common subject matter was still being consciously set forth in school-books, but by the 1950s that was no longer true: commonality in the early grades had collapsed.

claims.text: The how-to idea and the modern tradition of American education share a fundamental antipathy toward set curricula and factual knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Common to both is an antipathy to a set curriculum and “mere facts.”

claims.text: The educational movement commonly called 'Progressivism' is more accurately characterized as the 'anti-curriculum movement.'
claims.source_passage: Progressivism may have been the name the movement’s proponents liked to use, but it could more accurately be called “the anti-curriculum movement.”

claims.text: The amount of tacitly shared knowledge among the American population has decreased as a result of curricular changes.
claims.source_passage: Not surprisingly, tacitly shared knowledge among Americans has waned.

claims.text: The formal-skills approach to education wastes significant instructional time on unproductive drills.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, the anti-curriculum, formal-skills approach has wasted enormous amounts of school time in endless, unproductive drills.

claims.text: Child-centered educational approaches tend to bore children rather than engage them.
claims.source_passage: The child-centered idea... has led to an approach that, besides being unproductive, tends to bore children to distraction.

claims.text: Cognitive science indicates that a content-indifferent approach to education is destined for failure.
claims.source_passage: Cognitive science suggests that a content-indifferent approach to education cannot succeed

claims.text: The reading and writing skills of American high school graduates began a significant decline in the 1960s.
claims.source_passage: Starting in the 1960s, a decline in reading and writing skills of high school graduates began to be alarmingly apparent.

claims.text: The emphasis on reading skills under the No Child Left Behind (NCLB) policy has failed to reverse the decline in literacy scores.
claims.source_passage: Recently they have even declined somewhat further, despite the frantic emphasis on reading skills under No Child Left Behind.

claims.text: The primary cause of the decline in American test scores was the institutionalization of anti-curriculum ideas and skills-oriented textbooks in the 1950s.
claims.source_passage: The cause of the decline was not so much an influx of low-income students but an influx of educators trained in child-centered, anti-curriculum ideas, along with an influx of skills-oriented textbooks reflecting the anti-curriculum point of view.

claims.text: The state-standards movement failed to resolve the educational crisis because the creators of the standards were adherents of the anti-content tradition.
claims.source_passage: But the policy makers had not foreseen that those in charge of creating those standards would be guided by the very same child-centered, anti-set-content tradition that had brought on the crisis.

claims.text: Many state language-arts guidelines are 'monuments' to the anti-curriculum movement because they are vague, content-free, and interchangeable across grade levels.
claims.source_passage: Such empty guidelines (this one is from Arkansas) offer no guidance at all. The phrases could be copied and pasted into any grade level... They are monuments to the continuing triumph of the anti-curriculum movement.

claims.text: A cumulative core curriculum is a practical necessity for providing stability to low-income students who move frequently between schools.
claims.source_passage: “Would it be a good idea, considering the many low-income students who move from school to school, for there to be some commonality from one school to the next?” “Yes, definitely.”

claims.text: Current educational guidelines serve as monuments to the continued triumph of the anti-curriculum movement.
claims.source_passage: They are monuments to the continuing triumph of the anti-curriculum movement.

claims.text: Fostering a core of commonality in schooling is a concept that appeals to most Americans.
claims.source_passage: Fostering a core of commonality appeals to most Americans.

claims.text: A definite core curriculum is likely the only way schools can accomplish the goal of cumulative, year-by-year knowledge building.
claims.source_passage: And if they are asked, “Do you think a definite core curriculum is probably the only way schools can accomplish that goal?” the answer is a more reflective yes.

claims.text: American schools began losing their sense of urgent civic purpose in the 1930s due to the rise of new educational ideas.
claims.source_passage: gradually in the twentieth century a new set of ideas began to guide our schools, causing them by the 1930s, to start losing their sense of urgent civic purpose.

claims.text: By the 1950s, the chief mission of American schools had shifted from creating educated citizens to focusing on individual personal development.
claims.source_passage: By the 1950s they no longer conceived their chief mission to create educated citizens who shared a sense of public commitment and community. Their main emphasis shifted to the individual student’s personal development.

claims.text: The shift toward individual-focused schooling occurred because 20th-century Americans became overly optimistic that the Republic's stability was no longer at risk.
claims.source_passage: Rather, twentieth-century Americans had become optimistic about America. They no longer worried that the very stability and peace of the Republic hinged on diffusing shared knowledge and preparing virtuous, loyal citizens who would subordinate private aims to the good of the whole.

claims.text: By the turn of the 20th century, educators believed school learning should arise incidentally from concrete projects and 'hands-on' learning rather than book learning.
claims.source_passage: The needed knowledge would arise incidentally from immersion in concrete projects and “hands-on learning” rather than from deadly “rote memorization of mere facts.”

claims.text: The anti-bookish methods of American progressive education originated in the European Romantic movement of the 18th and 19th centuries.
claims.source_passage: this new, anti-bookish method was said to exemplify the superiority of American openness to the future—though it originated in writers of the European Romantic movement of the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries: Jean-Jacques Rousseau, Johann Heinrich Pestalozzi, and Friedrich Froebel.

claims.text: Overconfidence in the natural development of the individual child is fundamentally at odds with effective schooling.
claims.source_passage: Overconfidence in the natural development of the individual, these critics argued, was at odds with effective schooling.

claims.text: Individualistic schooling is at odds with the Founders' belief that the American experiment is fragile and requires vigilant education to survive.
claims.source_passage: We can now see that it was also at odds with the Founders’ and Lincoln’s sense of the fragility of the American experiment, whose perpetuation depends on the eternal vigilance of its schools and teachers.

claims.text: By the 1930s, individualistic, anti-subject-matter ideas already dominated American teacher-training institutions.
claims.source_passage: By the 1930s, the individualistic, anti-subject-matter ideas of child-centered schooling already dominated our teacher-training institutions

claims.text: The great weakness of Progressive Education is that it lacks a coherent theory of social welfare, tending instead toward anarchy or extreme individualism.
claims.source_passage: The great weakness of Progressive Education lies in the fact that it has elaborated no theory of social welfare, unless it be that of anarchy or extreme individualism.

claims.text: The claim that fulfilling the needs of the individual child automatically fulfills the needs of society is an unsubstantiated attempt to harmonize conflicting aims.
claims.source_passage: Educators began insisting that the fulfillment of the needs of the individual child and the fulfillment of the needs of society are inherently harmonious aims. All will be well, therefore, if the schools fulfill the needs of the individual child.

claims.text: Child-centered pedagogy has persisted for decades by adopting new terminological labels like 'constructivism' and 'critical thinking' while maintaining the same underlying ideology.
claims.source_passage: In American public schools since the 1930s, child-centered wine has been poured into ever-new terminological bottles—the “open classroom” in the 1970s, “constructivism” and “critical thinking” in the 1980s, and “Individual Learning Plans” in the 1990s.

claims.text: Republican government presupposes the existence of specific human qualities, such as esteem and confidence, in a higher degree than any other form of government.
claims.source_passage: Madison conceded the danger and the problem: 'As there is a degree of depravity in mankind which requires a certain degree of circumspection and distrust: So there are other qualities in human nature, which justify a certain portion of esteem and confidence. Republican government presupposes the existence of these qualities in a higher degree than any other form.'

claims.text: The decline in American verbal skills during the 1960s and 1970s cannot be attributed to an influx of low-income test takers, as demographically stable and wealthy populations like Iowa experienced identical declines.
claims.source_passage: Christopher Jencks... observed that during the 1960s and 1970s, the state of Iowa, like every other, suffered a steep decline in verbal scores, yet Iowa was then 98 percent white and middle class.

claims.text: Standard written and spoken Spanish is an artificial construction formed through dictionaries and school-promulgated knowledge assumptions, just like Standard American English.
claims.source_passage: Standard written and spoken Spanish, with its eighteenth-century dictionary and its school-promulgated knowledge assumptions, was just as artificially constructed as Standard American English.

claims.text: Contemporary American students are generally bright, idealistic, and working harder in school than students of the previous decade, despite their lack of general knowledge.
claims.source_passage: For the most part, these students are bright, idealistic, well meaning, and good-natured. Many are working harder in school than their siblings did a decade ago.

claims.text: The stability of the American political experiment, which protects the private sphere, is paradoxically dependent on a common public sphere that only schools can construct.
claims.source_passage: Like Madison and Lincoln, these New Yorkers understood that the American political experiment, which left everyone undisturbed in their private sphere, depended on a common public sphere that only the schools could create.

claims.text: Educational progressives in the early 20th century exhibited a 'deficient sense of tragedy' by relying too optimistically on the natural development of children.
claims.source_passage: One distinguished American theologian of the 1930s, Reinhold Niebuhr, complained that educational thinkers of the early twentieth century... exhibited a deficient sense of tragedy in their optimistic reliance on children’s natural development.

claims.text: The shared background knowledge required to understand public discourse is inherently 'inert' and slow to change, necessitating educational traditionalism.
claims.source_passage: I was forced to become an educational conservative. The tacit, intergenerational knowledge required to understand the language of newspapers, lectures, the Internet, and books in the library is inherently traditional and slow to change.

claims.text: Schooling in a democracy must prioritize the public sphere in the early grades regardless of individual talents or private associations.
claims.source_passage: In the early grades of schooling in a democracy, the public sphere should take priority. No matter what special talents and interests we may encourage in a young child, all of us have to learn the same base-ten system... the same twenty-six-letter alphabet.

evidence.description: Library of Congress photo (1893) of Miss Blanche Lamont’s classroom in Hecla, Montana, showing a diverse group of students in a remote location.
The standardized use of Noah Webster’s spelling books, William H. McGuffey’s Eclectic Readers, and Warren Colburn’s math books across the US.
Salma Hale’s 'History of the United States' which promoted principles of religious and political freedom.
The historical collapse of the ancient Roman Republic due to the rise of Caesars and internal factions.
Benjamin Franklin’s reply to a lady at the Constitutional Convention of 1787 regarding the form of government created.
James Madison's assertion in Federalist No. 55 regarding the necessary qualities of human nature for republican government.
Benjamin Rush's essay advocating for a common elementary curriculum to create 'republican machines.'
George Washington's estate bequest for education to spread systematic ideas and dissolve local prejudices.
Thomas Jefferson's plan for elementary schooling that mixed rich and poor students.
The 1852 Massachusetts law making common school compulsory.
The 1812 New York State Common School Act providing for a statewide system.
Abraham Lincoln's 1838 speech 'The Perpetuation of Our Political Institutions' regarding the teaching of the American creed.
Governor Silas Wright's 1845 address to the New York legislature linking school cultivation to the success of self-government.
The New York legislature's 1825 establishment of a fund for common textbooks in elementary schools.
Alexis de Tocqueville's observations of American civic loyalty and the role of education in 'Democracy in America'.
evidence.source_reference: Alexis de Tocqueville, Democracy in America (1831)

evidence.description: An interview with a young Muslim American woman on PBS NewsHour exhibiting 'American' linguistic traits despite religious dress.
NAEP (The Nation's Report Card) long-term trend data for 12th-grade reading, showing a gentle downward slope.
evidence.source_passage: One strong indication that the complaints of teachers are well founded is the long-term trend of the reading ability of twelfth graders, as documented by the National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP), 'The Nation’s Report Card.' The trend line is a gentle downward slope.

evidence.description: Implementation of Core Knowledge curriculum in a suburban Florida public school led by Connie Jones.
evidence.source_passage: in 1991 we began successful implementation in two public schools, one in suburban Florida led by principal Connie Jones

evidence.description: The growth of the Core Knowledge Foundation to over 1,000 schools in 47 states.
evidence.source_passage: Now there are more than a thousand Core Knowledge schools and preschools in forty-seven states.

evidence.description: Success of two initial Core Knowledge pilot schools in Florida and the South Bronx in 1991.
An experiment using a vague passage about laundry that subjects could not understand without a clarifying title.
A technical cricket-related passage from The Guardian that uses familiar words but is incomprehensible without specific background knowledge.
Declining or low reading test scores in the eighth and twelfth grades during the NCLB era.
Low and declining reading test scores in 8th and 12th grades despite the No Child Left Behind act.
The presence of 124 different editions of Hugh Blair's Rhetoric in the Harvard University library catalog.
Noah Webster's publication of 'Elements of Useful Knowledge' (1802-1812) to provide shared knowledge to Americans.
The success of William McGuffey’s 'Eclectic Readers' as grade-by-grade shared knowledge textbooks.
Horace Mann's 1848 annual report arguing that universal education creates social solidarity and reduces class antagonism.
Horace Mann's twelfth annual report (1848) regarding the role of education in merging property and labor into a single class.
The 1795 American Philosophical Society essay competition, where both winners (Knox and Smith) independently advocated for a national core curriculum.
Benjamin Rush's 1786 essay 'Thoughts upon the Mode of Education Proper in a Republic' arguing for homogeneity through schooling.
The success of national school systems in other parts of the world as a benchmark for high competence.
John Rawls' characterization of the United States as 'a social union of social unions.'
Richard Rorty's image of the 'club' and the 'bazaar' as a metaphor for the public-private distinction.
The New England model of private houses/churches surrounding a public commons.
The universal practice of modern nations imposing compulsory early education in basic skills and facts.
Robert Church and Michael Sedlak's historical account of the retreat from commonality in early 20th-century education.
evidence.source_reference: Church, R. and Sedlak, M. Education in the United States

evidence.description: A letter from a teacher in the New York Times arguing for critical thinking over facts.
evidence.source_reference: New York Times, 25th anniversary of A Nation at Risk

evidence.description: Comparison of current language-arts textbooks with historical ones (Webster, McGuffey) regarding shared knowledge content.
Scholastic Aptitude Test (SAT) verbal scores showing a peak in 1963 followed by a decline to a low point around 1990.
Research by Christopher Jencks showing that SAT verbal scores in Iowa declined despite the state being 98 percent white and middle class at the time.
The 1983 federal report 'A Nation at Risk' which characterized the state of American education as a threat to national security.
evidence.source_reference: National Commission on Excellence in Education (1983)

evidence.description: Analysis of current language-arts textbooks compared to historical texts like those of Noah Webster and William McGuffey.
Specific text from Arkansas state language-arts guidelines demonstrating vague, strategy-based instructions without specific content.
Public opinion polls showing general support for cumulative knowledge building and a core curriculum.
evidence.source_reference: Not explicitly cited in text snippet (ref 39, 40)

evidence.description: The presence of 'child-centered' labels in school mission statements and teacher training since the 1930s.

concepts.author_definition: A 19th-century educational institution designed to impart shared facts, myths, and values to create competent, loyal citizens regardless of location.
An informal but effective consensus among 19th-century schoolbook writers to standardize American curriculum in the absence of a national policy.
Internal conflicts and groups—based on ethnicity, region, religion, or economic status—that threaten the stability and common good of a republic.
A specific brand of citizen that subordinates local and personal interests to the common good, deemed necessary by the founders for the republic's survival.
Benjamin Rush's term for citizens who have been educationally prepared to prioritize the common good and republican virtues over local interests.
A shared domain created by schools where individuals from diverse private backgrounds and social strata can meet and interact as equals.
An educational institution centered on common knowledge, virtue, and skill, intended to create an allegiance to the larger community that transcends individual origins.
A form of education directed toward the public sphere and the maintenance of a democratic republic, contrasted with education aimed at fitting individuals for private life.
A state of cultural integration evidenced by shared linguistic intonations, vocabulary (argot), and mannerisms, which can coexist with distinct religious markers like a headscarf.

concepts.term: Civic theory of live and let live
concepts.author_definition: A historical political framework rooted in Enlightenment principles of toleration and church-and-state separation that allows diverse ethnic and religious groups to coexist harmoniously.
The unspoken, silently assumed information that a reader must already possess to comprehend a text.
Specific subject matter that is long-lasting and intergenerational, serving as the necessary foundation for shared communication and literacy.
A nonpartisan approach to school reform based on the scientific nature of reading and writing rather than ideological labels like 'left' or 'right.'
A mental model constructed by a reader or listener that makes textual elements fall into place and allows for necessary inferences of unstated information.
The large dimension of unspoken information shared between writer and reader that is essential for communication.
The lack of specific subject-matter background that prevents students from achieving reading comprehension, often tied to social class.
A scientifically flawed educational approach that treats reading as a set of formal skills rather than a knowledge-dependent activity.
The mastery of formal language codes and the 'tacit knowledge' (shared information) that makes communication within a society possible.
The practice of schools teaching a stable, intergenerational body of knowledge required for universal literacy.
The technical necessity of bringing children into a shared public speech community through common knowledge to enable effective learning and communication.
concepts.common_alternative: Often viewed as a purely political or ideological goal of cultural erasure.

concepts.author_definition: Artificial social barriers based on class or status that education can help to dissolve by creating a shared 'cultivated class.'
A lack of specific subject-matter knowledge in the curriculum, identified as a primary cause of school failure.
The domain of individual talent, private associations, and religious beliefs which is protected by a strong public sphere.
Rendering a diverse population shared in their public knowledge and civic identity to facilitate peaceable government, distinct from 'lock-step' personal uniformity.
A shared foundation of knowledge and values that fits the mass of people for uniform and peaceable government, distinct from personal talent.
concepts.common_alternative: Lock-step or standardized education that suppresses individual differences.

concepts.author_definition: A hierarchical model of knowledge starting with a common national core, followed by state and local knowledge, and finally individual student interests.
An educational model that places the individual child's interests, talents, and needs at the center, rather than a shared body of knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: A school focused on the development of the child rather than academic rigor.
concepts.source_passage: the new center was to be the individual child and his or her interests, talents, and needs. ... Schooling would no longer be subject-centered but would instead be child-centered.

concepts.author_definition: The theory that the primary purpose of school is to impart generalized skills (reading, writing, critical thinking) rather than specific facts or knowledge.
Hirsch's term for the modern American educational tradition combining child-centered and skills-based (how-to) ideas, characterized by an antipathy to a set curriculum.
concepts.source_passage: Progressivism may have been the name the movement’s proponents liked to use, but it could more accurately be called “the anti-curriculum movement.”

concepts.author_definition: The post-1983 policy effort where states agreed to set academic standards, which the author argues were ultimately captured by the anti-curriculum establishment.
An educational approach intended to engage child interest which, according to the author, actually results in boredom and lack of productivity when divorced from content.
An educational approach that prioritizes the individual student's personal development, interests, and activity over the systematic transmission of shared book knowledge.
The Romantic-era idea that education should follow the inherent, organic growth of the child rather than imposing external academic standards.
A metaphor for the public-private distinction where the bazaar represents the open public sphere and clubs represent diverse private associations.
John Rawls's definition of the United States as a collective entity made up of various private and social groupings.
A critique by Reinhold Niebuhr regarding the over-optimism of 20th-century educators who believed natural development alone would suffice for citizenship.
Textbooks designed to provide shared knowledge across a wide range of topics to enable intellectual competence and communication.

cases.name: Hecla, Montana (1893)
cases.description: A remote gold-rush town where teacher Blanche Lamont taught a standardized curriculum similar to what was being taught in large cities across America.
key_passages: All across the nation in 1893, whether in a quick-built gold-rush town like Hecla (now a ghost town) or in the multi-classroom schools of larger towns, American children were learning many of the same things.

cases.name: Ancient Rome
cases.description: A historical republic that failed because it could not control internal factions and self-seeking citizens, eventually falling to military dictatorship.
key_passages: The most famous example was the republic of ancient Rome, which was taken over by the unscrupulous Caesars and destroyed by what the American founders called “factions.”

cases.name: New York State (Early to Mid-19th Century)
cases.description: New York State, facing high diversity among immigrants and religious groups, intentionally used the common school system to build a shared domain for its citizens to prevent the kind of collapse seen in South American republics.
key_passages: New York State, with its diversity of immigrants and religious affiliations, was especially alert to the need to build up a shared domain where all these different groups could meet as equals on common ground.

The speeches of the state’s governors and state superintendents are filled with cautionary references to the South American republics, which were quickly collapsing into military dictatorships. Unless our schools created Americans, they warned, that would be our fate.

cases.name: New York State 1825 Textbook Reform
cases.description: The New York legislature established a fund to provide standardized textbooks (spellers, dictionaries, grammars, histories) to all elementary schools to ensure a common, non-partisan foundation for citizenship.
key_passages: the New York legislature established a fund to secure common textbooks for all of the state’s elementary schools
to make them American free men [and women] and American citizens, and to qualify them to judge and choose for themselves

cases.name: France's Headscarf Ban
cases.description: The French government's decision to bar Muslim girls from wearing headscarves in public schools, which Hirsch contrasts unfavorably with the American approach to assimilation.
key_passages: the ongoing controversy in France, where the government has barred Muslim girls from wearing head-scarves to school.
France—another democracy... is now making such a fuss about Muslim girls wearing headscarves to school.

cases.name: France headscarf controversy
cases.description: The French government's decision to ban Muslim girls from wearing headscarves in public schools. Hirsch contrasts this with the American approach, suggesting France has failed to learn from its own Enlightenment philosophers about toleration.
key_passages: the ongoing controversy in France, where the government has barred Muslim girls from wearing head-scarves to school.
I think that France has failed to learn important principles from its own philosophers and our brilliant American Founders.

cases.name: South Bronx Core Knowledge School
cases.description: One of the first implementations of the Core Knowledge curriculum in an urban, high-poverty environment. Led by Jeff Litt, the school achieved 'enormous success' but was ignored by the educational establishment.
key_passages: the other in the concrete wilderness of the South Bronx led by principal Jeff Litt. Both schools were enormously successful but—for reasons that I will explore later—largely ignored by educational experts

cases.name: Mohegan School and PS 124
cases.description: The initial 1991 implementations of the Core Knowledge curriculum in a suburban Florida school (led by Connie Jones) and a South Bronx school (led by Jeff Litt). Both were highly successful but ignored by education experts.
key_passages: in 1991 we began successful implementation in two public schools, one in suburban Florida led by principal Connie Jones, the other in the concrete wilderness of the South Bronx led by principal Jeff Litt. Both schools were enormously successful but—for reasons that I will explore later—largely ignored by educational experts

cases.name: 1795 American Philosophical Society Essay Contest
cases.description: An essay competition on the best education system for the US where winners Samuel Knox and Samuel Smith both advocated for a national core curriculum, demonstrating a contemporary consensus.
key_passages: In 1795, the members of the American Philosophical Society announced an essay competition on the subject of the best system of education 'adapted to the genius of the United States.' The prize was divided between two outstanding contestants, Samuel Knox and Samuel Smith, both of whose essays advocated a national core curriculum.

cases.name: The Era of Webster and McGuffey
cases.description: The period dominated by the textbooks of Webster and McGuffey, which Hirsch acknowledges as containing bigotry while simultaneously building the necessary public sphere of the early US.
key_passages: Such an aim would merely open an endless debate about the unfortunate way things really were during the WASPish days of Noah Webster and William McGuffey, whose schoolbooks served up significant doses of bigotry, pugnacious nationalism, and sectarian religiosity.

cases.name: The nineteenth-century common-school movement
cases.description: A movement that expanded on the ideas of the Founders (Jefferson, Rush, Webster) to establish a goal of preparing Americans for the public sphere through common educational content, despite local resistance to tax and control.
key_passages: The conviction that our schools need to make Americans out of all children... grew in strength in the nineteenth-century common-school movement.
The common-school movement succeeded in establishing the conscious goal of preparing Americans for the public sphere.

cases.name: The collapse of curriculum commonality (1940-1950)
cases.description: The period where common subject matter, which was still present in textbooks as late as 1940, disappeared by 1950, signaling the victory of the anti-curriculum movement.
key_passages: As late as 1940, some common subject matter was still being consciously set forth in school-books, but by the 1950s that was no longer true: commonality in the early grades had collapsed.

cases.name: Iowa (1960s-1970s)
cases.description: A case used by Christopher Jencks to isolate variables in the SAT score decline. Because Iowa was almost entirely white and middle class, the decline in its verbal scores proved that the national decline could not be blamed simply on an influx of low-income or minority test-takers.
key_passages: He observed that during the 1960s and 1970s, the state of Iowa, like every other, suffered a steep decline in verbal scores, yet Iowa was then 98 percent white and middle class.

cases.name: Arkansas State Guidelines
cases.description: A specific example of contemporary state education standards that focus on abstract reading strategies (e.g., 'predicting outcomes') rather than specific subject-matter knowledge.
key_passages: Such empty guidelines (this one is from Arkansas) offer no guidance at all. The phrases could be copied and pasted into any grade level, and in fact that is how many states’ language-arts guidelines are constructed.

cases.name: Progressive Education Association meeting of 1932
cases.description: A meeting where George Counts challenged the prevailing child-centered orthodoxy by arguing that progressive education failed to address the American social situation and lacked a theory of social welfare.
key_passages: George Counts, created a sensation in early 1932 at a meeting of the Progressive Education Association by telling his audience that if the new kind of child-centered education “is to fulfill its promise it must lose some of its easy optimism and prepare to deal more fundamentally, realistically, and positively with the American social situation.”

thinkers.name: James Madison
thinkers.key_idea: The danger of human 'depravity' and factionalism requires a republican government to cultivate higher human qualities than other systems.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Madison's Federalist No. 55 to argue that structural checks and balances are insufficient without a specific type of citizenry.
key_passages: Republican government presupposes the existence of these qualities in a higher degree than any other form.

thinkers.name: Benjamin Franklin
thinkers.key_idea: The American Republic's survival is not guaranteed and requires active maintenance by the people.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Franklin's famous 'A Republic, if you can keep it' anecdote to set the tone for the educational stakes.
key_passages: “Well, Doctor, what have we got?” to which Franklin famously replied: “A Republic, madam, if you can keep it.”

thinkers.name: Thomas Jefferson
thinkers.key_idea: Public education is the primary safeguard for the preservation of democratic ideals and the endurance of the republic.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch includes Jefferson among the founders who saw schools as the central hope for preventing republican collapse.
key_passages: Thomas Jefferson, John Adams, James Madison, and Franklin and their colleagues consistently alluded to the fact that republics have been among the least stable forms of government.

thinkers.name: Benjamin Rush
thinkers.key_idea: Schools should create 'republican machines' through a common elementary curriculum for all students.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Rush as a primary early advocate for the common core curriculum and the civic mission of education.
key_passages: The paramount aim of the schools, he wrote, was to create “republican machines.”

thinkers.name: Abraham Lincoln
thinkers.key_idea: To sustain the Union, schools must diligently teach the common American creed.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch highlights Lincoln’s 1838 speech to show that the precariousness of the republic necessitated a common educational curriculum.
key_passages: In order to sustain the Union, Lincoln said, parents, pastors, and schools must diligently teach the common American creed.

thinkers.name: Alexis de Tocqueville
thinkers.key_idea: American education successfully maintains the democratic republic by linking intellectual enlightenment with moral teaching and focusing on political life.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Tocqueville as a primary historical witness to the success of the 19th-century common school ideal.
key_passages: It cannot be doubted that in the United States the education of the people powerfully contributes to the maintenance of the democratic republic.

thinkers.name: John Maynard Keynes
thinkers.key_idea: Practical men are often the 'slaves' of defunct theorists without realizing it.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch applies Keynes's observation about economic policy to the field of education to argue that modern reformers are trapped by hidden, outdated theories.
key_passages: What John Maynard Keynes said about economic policy applies equally to education: 'Practical men who believe themselves to be quite exempt from any intellectual influence are usually the slaves of some defunct [theorist].'

thinkers.name: American Founders
thinkers.key_idea: The political genius of toleration and church-state separation created at the height of the Enlightenment.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch credits the Founders with creating a 'civic theory' that allows for multi-ethnic harmony, which he argues is currently being neglected in schools.
key_passages: thanks to the political genius of America’s Founders, together with the accident that they lived at the height of the Enlightenment, when principles of toleration and church-and-state separation were at the forefront of advanced thought.

thinkers.name: Hugh Blair
thinkers.key_idea: Published 'Lectures on Rhetoric and Belles Lettres' (1783) to provide Americans and Britons with the shared knowledge needed to communicate in the public sphere.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Blair as a historical precedent for the idea that effective communication requires a specific body of shared background knowledge.
key_passages: Hugh Blair also had this idea of shared background knowledge in view when he published his Lectures on Rhetoric and Belles Lettres in 1783—informing both Britons and Americans what they needed to know... to communicate in the public sphere.

thinkers.name: Noah Webster
thinkers.key_idea: Established American standard language and published 'Elements of Useful Knowledge' to teach shared tacit knowledge to all Americans.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Webster to show that the founders of American education explicitly intended to create a shared knowledge base to unify the nation.
key_passages: early schoolbook writers like Noah Webster understood the need... to teach the tacit knowledge, shared by everyone in the public sphere.

thinkers.name: William McGuffey
thinkers.key_idea: Created 'Eclectic Readers' based on the principle that intellectual competence requires shared knowledge across varied topics.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch points to McGuffey's readers as successful historical models of grade-by-grade knowledge-based curriculum.
key_passages: He called them “eclectic” because, like Blair and Webster, he knew that effective communication and intellectual competence require shared knowledge over a wide range of topics.

thinkers.name: Horace Mann
thinkers.key_idea: Universal education expand the 'cultivated class' to create social solidarity and eliminate 'factitious distinctions' such as class and caste.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Mann to support the normative claim that shared knowledge is the essential ingredient for social harmony and democracy.
key_passages: Horace Mann understood that shared knowledge is essential to our sense of solidarity and harmonious well-being.

thinkers.name: John Rawls
thinkers.key_idea: The United States is 'a social union of social unions.'
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Rawls to illustrate the social nature of private associations within the 'private sphere.'
key_passages: Political philosopher John Rawls has called us “a social union of social unions.”

thinkers.name: Richard Rorty
thinkers.key_idea: The image of 'private clubs' facing out on a 'big, open public bazaar.'
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Rorty's metaphor to visualize the distinction between diverse private lives and a shared public space.
key_passages: Philosopher Richard Rorty offered the “club” and the “bazaar” as an alternative image for the public-private distinction

thinkers.name: Robert Church and Michael Sedlak
thinkers.key_idea: The 'Retreat from Commonality' in early twentieth-century education.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites their historical work to document when the common school movement began to fade.
key_passages: “The Retreat from Commonality” is the heading of part 3 of Education in the United States, by Robert Church and Michael Sedlak

thinkers.name: Christopher Jencks
thinkers.key_idea: The decline in SAT scores in the 1960s and 70s was caused by changes in schools and textbooks in the 1950s, not by demographic changes in test-takers.
thinkers.engagement: The author uses Jencks' sociological research to refute the common demographic explanation for falling literacy and to point instead to the 'anti-curriculum' training of educators.
key_passages: Christopher Jencks, who studies social policy at Harvard, has shown that the explanation is inadequate.
The cause of the decline was not so much an influx of low-income students but an influx of educators trained in child-centered, anti-curriculum ideas

thinkers.name: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
thinkers.key_idea: Romantic naturalist education focused on the child's nature rather than academic content.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch identifies him as a primary source of the 'anti-bookish' and individualistic methods that undermined the American common school.
key_passages: though it originated in writers of the European Romantic movement of the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries: Jean-Jacques Rousseau

thinkers.name: Reinhold Niebuhr
thinkers.key_idea: Criticized the 'deficient sense of tragedy' and over-optimism in educational reliance on natural development.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Niebuhr's theological critique to support the claim that the educational optimism of the early 20th century was naive and dangerous.
key_passages: Reinhold Niebuhr, complained that educational thinkers of the early twentieth century... exhibited a deficient sense of tragedy in their optimistic reliance on children’s natural development.

thinkers.name: George Counts
thinkers.key_idea: Argued that child-centered education was 'too narrow' and resulted in extreme individualism or anarchy because it ignored the needs of society.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Counts to show that even within the progressive movement, some recognized the danger of losing the school's social and civic purpose.
key_passages: The great weakness of Progressive Education lies in the fact that it has elaborated no theory of social welfare, unless it be that of anarchy or extreme individualism.
